pattern: A mirror match is discovered from the later index's point of view (does some earlier value's reverse equal me?), so scanning once while remembering the most recent index that produced each possible reversed value turns every match into an O(1) lookup.
hints: For a pair (i, j) to mirror, reverse(nums[i]) must equal nums[j] -- so think of it from j's side: 'has some earlier index produced a reversal equal to my own value?'

While scanning left to right, keep a map from a reversed value to the most recent index whose reversal produced it; check the current value against that map *before* recording the current index's own reversal.

Because you always want the smallest gap, only the most recent index for each reversed value ever matters -- overwriting the map with the newest index as you go is always at least as good as keeping an older one.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, two separate mirror pairs
statement example, trailing zero disappears on reversal
statement example, no mirror pair exists
single element can never form a pair
a palindromic number mirrors itself at the next index
several trailing zeros collapse on reversal to a much smaller number
two elements at the maximum value that do not mirror
closest possible mirror pair sits at adjacent indices further down the array

stress.description: large random array across the full value range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

complexity.time: O(n) amortized (reversal costs O(digits) per element)
